questions.question: State and prove Lagrange's Theorem. If |G| = 24 and |H| = 8, find the number of left cosets of H in G.
keyPoints: State: For any finite group G and subgroup H, |H| divides |G|
Proof outline: Cosets partition G, each coset has |H| elements
Number of cosets = index [G:H] = |G|/|H|
Number of left cosets = 24/8 = 3

questions.question: Define: (i) Order of a group (ii) Order of an element (iii) Index of a subgroup. If G is a group of order 12, what are the possible orders of elements in G?
keyPoints: (i) Order = number of elements in group
(ii) Order of element = smallest n: g^n = e
(iii) Index = number of cosets of H in G
Possible orders must divide 12: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12
By Cauchy's Theorem, primes 2 and 3 definitely appear

questions.question: What is a cyclic group? Show that every cyclic group is abelian. Find all generators of Z_12.
keyPoints: Cyclic group: generated by single element
Abelian proof: g^a · g^b = g^(a+b) = g^b · g^a
Z_12 generators: elements coprime to 12: 1, 5, 7, 11
Verify: 5(1)=5, 5(2)=10, 5(3)=3, ..., generates all elements

questions.question: Explain the concept of normal subgroup. Prove that every subgroup of an abelian group is normal.
keyPoints: Normal subgroup: gH = Hg for all g ∈ G
Proof: For abelian G, gh = hg for all g,h ∈ G
Take any g ∈ G, h ∈ H: g·h = h·g ∈ Hg = gH
Thus gH ⊆ Hg and similarly Hg ⊆ gH
Therefore gH = Hg, so H is normal

questions.question: State Cauchy's Theorem. How does it relate to Lagrange's Theorem? Give an example of a group where not every divisor of |G| corresponds to a subgroup.
keyPoints: Cauchy: If p prime divides |G|, then G has element of order p
Relation: Cauchy's Theorem is partial converse to Lagrange's
Example: A_4 has order 12 but no subgroup of order 6
Divisors of 12 are 1,2,3,4,6,12 but 6 is missing
This shows Lagrange's converse is false in general

questions.question: Define quotient group. If G = Z_12 and N = {0, 3, 6, 9}, find the order of the quotient group G/N.
keyPoints: Quotient group G/N = set of cosets of normal subgroup N
N is normal as Z_12 is abelian
|N| = 4 (elements listed)

questions.question: Explain left and right cosets with an example. Prove that any two cosets of H in G are either disjoint or equal.
keyPoints: Left coset: gH = {gh | h ∈ H}
Right coset: Hg = {hg | h ∈ H}
Example in Z_6: H = {0,3}, g=1 gives {1,4}, g=2 gives {2,5}
Proof: If g₁H ∩ g₂H ≠ ∅, let x ∈ intersection
Then x = g₁h₁ = g₂h₂, so g₂ = g₁h₁h₂⁻¹ ∈ g₁H
Thus g₂H ⊆ g₁H, similarly g₁H ⊆ g₂H, so equal

questions.question: Let G be a group of order 2n where n is odd. Prove that G has a subgroup of order n. Use this to show that every group of order 2n (n odd) has a normal subgroup of index 2.
keyPoints: Consider element a of order 2 (exists by Cauchy for p=2)
Let H = ⟨a⟩, so |H| = 2
By Lagrange, [G:H] = |G|/|H| = (2n)/2 = n (odd)
Key: Show H is normal by showing left and right cosets equal
Or: Show any subgroup of index 2 is normal
Alternative: If G has element of order n, that subgroup is index 2 and normal
Index 2 subgroups are always normal: gH and H are the only cosets

questions.question: (a) State and prove Lagrange's Theorem. (b) Show that the order of every element in a finite group divides the order of the group. (c) In a group of order 25, what are the possible orders of subgroups?
keyPoints: (a) State: |H| divides |G| for H ≤ G
Proof: Cosets partition G, each coset has |H| elements
Number of cosets = |G|/|H| = index
(b) Order of element g = subgroup ⟨g⟩
By Lagrange, |⟨g⟩| divides |G|
Therefore order of g divides |G|
(c) Divisors of 25 are 1, 5, 25
Possible subgroup orders: 1, 5, 25
By Cauchy, subgroup of order 5 exists since 5 divides 25

questions.question: Define: (i) Cyclic group (ii) Generator of a group (iii) Normal subgroup. Show that if G is a cyclic group of order n, then for each divisor d of n, there exists exactly one subgroup of order d.
keyPoints: (i) Cyclic: G = ⟨g⟩ = {g^k | k ∈ Z}
(ii) Generator: element that generates entire group
(iii) Normal: gN = Ng for all g ∈ G
Proof: Let d divide n, want subgroup of order d
Let g be generator of order n
Consider h = g^(n/d)
Then h has order d (verify: h^d = g^n = e, and smaller doesn't work)
Uniqueness: Any subgroup of order d is cyclic, generated by element of order d
All elements of order d are powers of g^(n/d), so unique

questions.question: (a) Prove that every subgroup of a cyclic group is cyclic. (b) State the Fundamental Theorem of Finite Abelian Groups (basic version). (c) Find all subgroups of Z_12 and draw its subgroup lattice.
keyPoints: (a) Let G = ⟨g⟩ be cyclic, H ≤ G
If H = {e}, trivial and cyclic
Otherwise, let m be smallest positive integer with g^m ∈ H
Claim: H = ⟨g^m⟩
Proof: any h ∈ H = g^k, divide k = mq + r, 0 ≤ r < m
g^r = g^(k-mq) ∈ H, contradict minimality if r > 0
So r = 0, h = (g^m)^q, thus h ∈ ⟨g^m⟩
(b): Every finite abelian group is isomorphic to direct product of cyclic groups of prime power order, uniquely determined by invariant factors
Subgroups: ⟨0⟩ = {0} (order 1), ⟨6⟩ = {0,6} (order 2)
